item.title: Getting Started with Astro
item.content:encoded:
Astro is a modern static site generator that delivers lightning-fast performance by shipping zero JavaScript by default.

Why Astro?

There are many reasons to choose Astro for your next project:

  • Performance first — Astro ships zero JS by default
  • Content-focused — Built for content-rich websites
  • Island architecture — Hydrate interactive components on demand

Getting Started

To create a new Astro project, run:

npm create astro@latest

Then follow the prompts. It's that easy!

Project Structure

A typical Astro project looks like this:

src/
  pages/
  components/
  layouts/
  content/
public/
astro.config.mjs

Check out the official documentation for more details.
item.excerpt:encoded: A quick guide to getting started with Astro, the modern static site generator that ships zero JavaScript by default.

item.title: Tailwind CSS v4: What's New
item.content:encoded:
Tailwind CSS v4 brings a completely new engine built on top of Lightning CSS, along with a redesigned configuration experience.

Key Changes

CSS-First Configuration

Gone is the tailwind.config.js file. In v4, you configure everything directly in CSS using the @theme directive:

@import "tailwindcss";

@theme {
  --color-brand: #7c3aed;
  --font-heading: "Inter", sans-serif;
}

Performance Improvements

The new engine is 10x faster than v3 for full builds and over 100x faster for incremental builds. This means near-instant HMR in development.

Migration Tips

  1. Remove your tailwind.config.js file
  2. Move theme customizations to @theme blocks in CSS
  3. Update any custom plugins to the new API

The migration is mostly straightforward, but test thoroughly!
item.excerpt:encoded: Exploring the major changes in Tailwind CSS v4, including the new CSS-first configuration and massive performance improvements.

item.title: Draft: My Thoughts on Web Components
item.content:encoded:
Web Components have been around for years, but adoption has been slow. Here are my unfiltered thoughts on the state of the platform in 2025.

The Good

Shadow DOM encapsulation is genuinely useful for design systems and widget libraries. The customElements.define() API is simple enough, and with declarative shadow DOM landing in all major browsers, SSR is finally viable.

The Not-So-Good

The developer experience still lags behind frameworks like React, Vue, and Svelte. Templating with template literals feels clunky, and reactivity requires manual wiring.

The best technology is the one your team can ship with confidently.

I'll expand on this more once I've gathered my full thoughts.

item.title: About Page
item.content:encoded: This is a page, not a post. It should be skipped by the migration script.
